text: The robotic esophagectomy is performed in the prone position using two ports. A monopolar scissor is inserted through a special port, while a bipolar scissor is used on the left arm. The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus along the infra azygos posterior cut, carefully dissecting and coagulating vessels. The thin layer of pleura is cut, with meticulous attention to avoid thoracic duct injury.

The surgeon performs an anterior dissection along the pulmonary vessels, carefully mobilizing the esophagus without direct handling to prevent damage. Monopolar scissors are used for precise dissection along the anterior border, reaching the lower esophagus without opening the hiatus. The left bronchus, carina, and right bronchus are visible, with caution taken to avoid injuring the trachea and bronchus. A supra-azygous cut is made posteriorly, followed by mobilization towards the neck. Hemlock clips are applied to the azygous vein before cutting, aiding in nodal dissection near the recurrent line.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the bronchial artery, while the esophagus is mobilized from the suprasegos and trasegos regions, away from the liver. The dissection progresses slowly, exposing the bronchus and carina. Care is taken to avoid injuring the thoracic duct, and minimal cautery is used to prevent conduction current damage. The bent monopolar scissor aids in precise dissection during robotic surgery.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to dissect the esophagus and bring it out to the left side near the outer pulmonary window. The hiatus is dissected but not opened. The vagus nerve is identified, and the recurrent nerve is located near the right bronchial nodes. The nodes are carefully dissected with minimal cautery to prevent damage, and they are packed in a plastic cover to prevent contamination.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the right Arlan nodes, removing lymph nodes up to the neck side. Subcranial nodal dissection is performed with care to avoid proximity to the carina, using bipolar cautery. The left bronchial nodes are dissected and removed, followed by dissection of the left recurrent angina nodes, which are bagged into a plastic pouch. The left recurrent angina is visible, and dissection continues up to the neck side, avoiding direct use of scissors. The outer pulmonary nodes are removed, and the patient is turned into a supine position.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to immobilize the greater and lesser omentum. Lymph nodes labeled as 8A and 8B are dissected out near the splenic artery, with the third arm retracting the stomach. The left gastric vein is coagulated and cut, and the left gastric nodes are dissected. The robotic instruments, including scissors and bipolar cautery, are used to dissect the left gastric artery and lymph nodes, adhering to oncological principles. The stomach is cleared, and an automated stapler is used to create a gastric conduit, with the neck immobilized for the procedure.
